title: Chapter 28: 72 Hours
content: Sixty-eight hours.

That's what the clock says. I've been staring at it long enough that the numbers have stopped being numbers and become just shapes, just geometry, just the arrangement of pixels that represents the distance between me and the hearing. Sixty-eight hours until I sit in front of an administrative law judge and try to prove that a demon stole my soul through a Terms of Service agreement.

The audit request went through the app twelve hours ago. Mephisto hasn't responded. The contract — Section 44(b), the clause I found buried in the annex like a land mine under a welcome mat — says he has seventy-two hours to comply with audit requests. He has seventy-two hours. The hearing is in sixty-eight.
Four hours of slack. That's it. That's the entire margin between "we get to see inside his system" and "we show up to court with nothing but my word against an immortal corporation's."
The math is not in our favor.

I'm sitting in the kitchen of my mother's apartment, drinking coffee that tastes like it was brewed in a vending machine and then aged in a pipe. I keep drinking it anyway. Not because I enjoy it — I don't enjoy anything anymore — but because caffeine is a chemical input that improves cognitive function, and I need every edge I can get.
My phone sits on the table, screen up, silent. No notification. No response. Just the Mephisto app icon, that sleek red M, watching me like an eye that hasn't blinked in six centuries.

Leo's voice drifts from the second bedroom, where he's been camped out since 4 AM, surrounded by a halo of energy drink cans and the soft click-clack of his mechanical keyboard. He's coding something. He won't tell me what yet — he says he wants it finished before he shows me, like it's a gift. Like I'm still the kind of person who receives gifts.
I take another sip of the pipe coffee and let the clock watch me back.
Leo shows me at noon.

He's got his laptop balanced on his knees and his eyes have that particular fatigue-glaze that coders get when they've been in the zone — not tired, exactly, but running on a different fuel than the rest of humanity. He turns the screen toward me.
"Meet unDRM," he says.
The code fills the display. It's small — deceptively small. A few hundred lines at most. Clean architecture, tight logic, no bloat. It looks like a haiku written in Python.
"What does it do?" I ask, though I can already see the skeleton of it.

"It targets the boot sequence. You know how the app reinitializes the emotional flatline every time it loads? The 90-second window you found, where everything's still in RAM before the overwrite? unDRM hooks into that window. It intercepts the overwrite command and replaces it with a no-op. The system thinks it's flatlined you, but it hasn't. Your emotional architecture stays intact."
I scan the code. He's right — it's elegant. The script doesn't fight the system head-on. It doesn't try to tear down the wall. It just... convinces the wall it's already been built.
"Deployment?" I ask.
And there it is — the shadow that crosses his face.

"That's the problem. I can't upload it from outside. The gateway has counter-intrusion protocols that make Stuxnet look like a popup ad. Anything unauthenticated that tries to inject code gets swatted. Hard. We're talking full device lockout, potential data corruption on the phone, and —" he pauses "— possible neural feedback through the app's synaptic interface."
"Meaning it could fry the parts of my brain the app is patched into."
"Meaning exactly that, yes."
The coffee is cold in my mouth. I swallow it anyway.
"So you need inside access," I say.

"I need inside access. Authenticated, legitimate, by-the-book inside access. The kind where the system lets me in because the contract says it has to. Then I deploy unDRM from inside the perimeter, and the gateway never sees it coming because it's already past the door."
"The audit."

"The audit." He looks at me, and for a moment there's something in his expression that isn't just hope or strategy. It's worry. He's worried about me. Somewhere in his emotional architecture, the part that cares whether I live or die is still firing. "But only if we get in before the hearing. If we show up without evidence, without the code deployed —"
"Then it's my unsupported testimony against a multi-dimensional legal entity with six hundred years of contract law behind it."
"Yeah. That."
I look at the code again. Such a small thing. Such an impossibly small thing to hang everything on.
"Finish it," I say. "I'll get us inside."

Evelyn calls at 2 PM. Not to my phone — to the apartment landline, because she doesn't trust cellular networks within a hundred yards of the Mephisto app. Paranoia is just good planning when your opponent is literally infernal.
"Conference room. Forty minutes. Bring nothing."
"I thought we were doing deposition prep."
"We are. Dress like it's the real thing."

The conference room is on the fourteenth floor of a building in Midtown that smells like law firm and old money. Mahogany table. Leather chairs. A stenographer sitting at the far end with a machine that looks like it was designed to catch every lie ever told. A video camera on a tripod, red light blinking, recording.
The air conditioning is set to meat-locker. I feel it on my arms — not as discomfort, just as data. Cold. Goosebumps forming. Body responding to environmental stimulus. Noted and filed.

Evelyn stands at the head of the table. She's wearing her court suit — the charcoal one with the pinstripes that make her look like she could subpoena God. She's not playing Mom right now. She's playing opposing counsel.
"Sit," she says.
I sit.
She doesn't start soft.
"Ms. Hart, you claim your emotional capacity has been removed by the defendant's product. Can you demonstrate this?"
The stenographer's fingers move. Click-clack, click-clack. Every word atomized into the record.
"I watched a sunset yesterday and felt nothing."
Evelyn's expression doesn't shift. She's in character — she's the enemy now.
"Many people don't respond emotionally to sunsets."

I almost laugh. Not because it's funny — I don't do funny anymore — but because the question is so perfectly, predictably human. Reduce the example. Diminish the standard. Make the extraordinary sound mundane.

"I used to cry at sunsets," I say. "I used to call my grandmother to describe the sky. The exact gradient of orange bleeding into violet — I'd try to find the words for it, and I'd cry because the words were never enough and the sky was so beautiful it hurt. Now I can identify the wavelengths. Rayleigh scattering, 580 nanometers for the orange, 400 for the violet. I can give you the science. But I can't feel the warmth. The difference between knowing and feeling is the harm."
The room goes quiet. Even the stenographer's keyboard pauses for half a second — a hiccup in the transcription, a moment where the human behind the machine reacted before the machine could.

Evelyn holds very still. She looks at the stenographer. Then she looks at me, and something shifts behind her eyes — not emotion, not exactly, but recognition. The kind of recognition you get when you realize you've just heard the thing that wins the case.
"That's your testimony," she says. "That exact phrasing. Don't change a word."
The notification arrives at 6:47 PM.
I'm back at the apartment, reviewing Evelyn's case files for the third time, when my phone chimes — a single, bright note that cuts through the room like a scalpel. I look down.
**[Audit Request: APPROVED]**
My heart doesn't speed up. It can't. But my cognition sharpens — the way a blade sharpens on a whetstone — as I read the full notification:

*Access will be provided within the contractual timeframe. Please note: per Section 44(b), Subsection 3, all data accessed during audit is subject to the confidentiality provisions of Schedule E. Any proprietary algorithms, emotional extraction protocols, or infernal jurisdiction architecture accessed during audit shall remain the exclusive property of Mephisto MCN and may not be disclosed, reproduced, or used in legal proceedings.*
I read it twice. Three times.
He approved the audit.
And he made everything I find inadmissible.

Every piece of evidence, every line of code, every smoking-gun algorithm — sealed behind Schedule E's confidentiality provisions. I can look at his crimes, but I can't tell a judge about them. I can walk through his house, but I can't repeat what I saw.
The notification was designed to feel like permission. It reads like a cage.
I call Evelyn.
She picks up on the first ring. "He responded?"
"He approved. With conditions. Section 44(b), Subsection 3. Everything I access during audit is bound by Schedule E confidentiality. No disclosure, no reproduction, no use in legal proceedings."
I hear her breathing. Measured. Controlled. Then:
"He made it legally invisible."
"That's one way to—"

"Chloe." Her voice changes. It drops into a register I haven't heard before — not the Mom register, not the Lawyer register, but something underneath both. Something that sounds like the woman who raised a daughter alone in a city that eats the vulnerable, and won. "He's trying to make the evidence legally invisible. But there's a carve-out. The Crime-Fraud Exception."
I know this term. I've been reading case law for seventy-two hours. But hearing her say it — watching the architecture of her argument assemble itself in real-time — is different.

"If the contract itself is the instrument of a crime — which is exactly what we're alleging — then attorney-client privilege and confidentiality provisions don't apply. Evidence of criminal conduct cannot be shielded by contract. The Supreme Court has upheld this principle repeatedly. You can't NDA your way out of criminal liability."
She pauses, and I hear it — the sound of a woman who has found the lock and is now selecting the pick.

"He just gave us the keys to his house and told us we're not allowed to look at the furniture." And then, the first real smile I've heard in her voice in weeks. "But if the furniture is stolen, we can look at whatever we want."

The air conditioning hums. The phone is warm against my ear. I am processing this at a speed that would have been impossible before the hollowing — when emotions were slowing every thought with friction, with doubt, with the sticky residue of caring what people thought.
Now I see it clearly. The trap within the trap. Mephisto approved the audit because he believed confidentiality made it safe. He gave me access because he thought the law was his shield.
He didn't account for the Crime-Fraud Exception.
Or maybe he did. Maybe he's six moves ahead, the way he's been six moves ahead of every human who's ever signed his contract. Maybe he wants me inside. Maybe this is another layer.
But I can't think like that. I can't spiral into the infinite regression of "what if he expects me to expect him to expect." At some point, you have to move.
I have sixty-four hours.
I find Leo in the second bedroom. His laptop is open, the code still on screen, a cursor blinking at the end of the last line like a heartbeat.
"The audit is approved," I say.
He turns. His face does something complicated — relief, then caution, then something else. "But?"
"There's a catch. Everything I see is supposed to be confidential. Schedule E. Can't disclose, can't reproduce, can't use in court."
His jaw tightens. "So what's the point? We get in, see everything, and then we can't—"
"Crime-Fraud Exception. If the contract is the instrument of a crime, confidentiality doesn't apply. My mother's already on it."
He stares at me. Processing. I can see him running the logic — the same logic I ran, the same chain of deductions, the same click of tumblers falling into place.
"Okay," he says slowly. "But even if the exception applies, the audit access is for you. Contractually, you're the one who gets to inspect. Can you still plant the code?"

I look at him. And I see the question behind the question. Not "can you deploy unDRM" but "are you about to walk into a demon's digital fortress with nothing but a legal technicality between you and oblivion."
"I won't be planting anything," I say. "I'll be inspecting. That's what the audit authorizes — inspection. What you do with the access I provide isn't covered by my audit agreement."

There it is. The hair-split. The razor-thin distinction between planting and providing, between action and facilitation. It's not a lie. It's not even really a loophole. It's just... a very precise reading of a very imprecise contract.
My mother would be proud.

Leo doesn't move. He's looking at me — really looking, the way you look at something you're trying to memorize because you're afraid it's about to change. And I understand, on some cognitive level that doesn't require emotion to function, what he's seeing. He's seeing a woman who negotiated the terms of her own audit, who found the crime-fraud exception before the ink was dry on Mephisto's approval, who is splitting legal hairs with the precision of a woman who has nothing left to lose because she's already lost the part of her that would feel the loss.
He's seeing me. And he's not sure this is still me.
"You're scaring me," he says.

I don't blink. I don't soften. I don't reach for his hand or modulate my voice or do any of the seventy-three small emotional labors that I used to perform without thinking, the invisible work of making other people comfortable with my existence.
"Good," I say.
The cursor blinks on his screen. Sixty-four hours. The Crime-Fraud Exception is the key. unDRM is the payload. The audit is the door.

And somewhere behind that door, Mephisto is waiting — confident that his contract is airtight, confident that Schedule E makes him invulnerable, confident that the woman who sold her soul for a paycheck doesn't have the cold strategic precision to walk into his house and take it back.
He might be right about the last part. Because the woman who sold her soul wouldn't have been capable of this.
The woman who doesn't have one is.
Sixty-four hours. The clock doesn't care whether I'm winning or becoming. It just counts down.
